calls.key_strengths:
1. Good rapport and natural conversation flow — Sophie was comfortable throughout.
2. Co-parenting dynamic identified and respected early.
3. Clean follow-up locked in with no pressure — Monday callback scheduled.
calls.critical_misses:
1. When Sophie mentioned her own teenage struggles ('it brings back all those memories') — the emotional peak of the call — it was barely acknowledged before moving on.
2. Pain stack built in Anthony's words, not Sophie's — 'lost', 'afraid', 'don't know what direction' were Sophie's exact words and weren't used.
3. At 7-8/10 with clear interest, should have tried harder to get dad on the call now, or locked in a stronger conditional commitment.
calls.one_drill: When a parent mentions their own history: 'It sounds like this is hitting close to home for you, Sophie. Tell me — what were your teenage years like?' Then mirror Leo's situation back through her experience. This unlocks the deepest emotional purchase.
calls.one_line: "Sophie, it sounds like watching Leo struggle is bringing up your own teenage stuff — and that's the scariest part, right?"

calls.key_strengths:
1. Excellent surface — Matthew's autism/ADHD/sexuality/online danger surfaced richly in the opening 3 minutes.
2. Very efficient, confident program pitch — NDIS option offered proactively at the right moment.
3. Clean close — Leanne committed on-call, portal walkthrough completed despite tech issues.
calls.critical_misses:
1. Leanne was pre-sold — Anthony matched her low emotional temperature instead of deepening it. Depth was available.
2. 'It sounds like it's been a pretty stressful time' — generic label used across calls; needs specificity per parent.
3. Matthew's father passing was mentioned and then not explored for emotional impact — a rare and significant moment.
calls.one_drill: Practice naming the specific emotion after a parent discloses a loss or trauma. After 'his dad passed away a few years ago': pause, then 'Leanne, can I check in — Matthew lost his dad. What does that add to all of this for him? And for you?'
calls.one_line: "Leanne, it sounds like you've been fighting this battle completely alone — is that fair to say?"

calls.key_strengths:
1. Excellent objection isolation — 'if dad wasn't on board, what would happen?' perfectly isolated the conditional objection before addressing it.
2. Strong 1-10 scale execution — achieved a 10/10 and used the momentum cleanly.
3. Smooth close execution — portal walkthrough, activation pass, W1 booked for March 27.
calls.critical_misses:
1. The suicide comment ('I worry about young men with suicide') was the emotional peak of the call — Anthony moved past it far too quickly without sitting in it.
2. Emotional labels present but not precision-targeted — 'It sounds like it's becoming a lot for you' is too vague when the underlying fear is 'I'm terrified I'll lose him'.
3. Didn't probe why Jack chose to move to dad's house — rich surface/impact goldmine left unexplored.
calls.one_drill: The pause after the big line — when a parent mentions suicide risk, next move is silence, then: 'Tell me more about that, Judith.' Practice sitting in that silence for 3 full seconds before responding.
calls.one_line: "Judith, it sounds like deep down you're terrified of losing him — is that right?"

calls.key_strengths:
1. Exceptional surface work — uncovered full DV history, brothers in jail, 7-year foster journey in first 5 mins.
2. Strong rapport — Beck opened up completely, shared everything without prompting.
3. Good program walkthrough, appropriately simplified for a skeptical/exhausted parent.
calls.critical_misses:
1. Emotional labelling was generic ('wearing a fair bit of weight') — never named the core emotion (trapped, exhausted, grieving).
2. Impact not cycled — 'can't work because of him' and 'choosing between Brax and my biological kids' were huge moments left on the table.
3. Price objection: sent link before re-anchoring to pain. Should have cycled impact THEN presented price.
calls.one_drill: Pain cycling drill — after Beck says something impactful, stop and ask: 'What does that feel like for you as a foster mum?' or 'How is that affecting you day to day?' Don't let big pain moments pass without cycling.
calls.one_line: "Before I talk price, I want to make sure I've heard you, Beck — does it feel like you're having to choose between saving him and protecting your own family?"

calls.key_strengths: Exceptional situational discovery — deepest story uncovered of all 5 calls. Objection handling excellent — offered to personally engage Tyson to reduce his resistance. Genuinely empathetic tone throughout. 'Load lifted' closing moment shows real emotional connection made.
calls.critical_misses: Opening frame weak and confusing — 'the point of this call, Joe, really obviously is not...' unclear. Emotional labeling generic — missed naming Jo's abandonment, grief, isolation as specific emotions. Impact not cycled back to create urgency. BAMFAM close leaves the deal hanging — 'I'll be in touch this afternoon' without a firmer commitment or start fee collected on call.
calls.one_drill: Abandonment cycling drill: 'Jo, Tyson said this morning ‘if he was so worried about me, why would he leave me?’ — that's not a question, that's a wound. Before we move on, tell me what that was like to hear from your son.'
calls.one_line: When a 15-year-old asks why his dad left him, that's not a conversation about the program — that's the whole reason you're here. Stay there.

calls.key_strengths: Good casual rapport at opening. Clean frame set. Leo's interests surfaced (surfing, skateboarding). Follow-up booked for Monday.
calls.critical_misses: MASSIVE miss: Sophie disclosed personal teenage trauma — not followed up at all. Emotional labeling barely present. Impact on Sophie's daily life never fully explored. Pain stack essentially empty. Went straight to $111 multiple times without addressing 'dad needs to agree' properly. Leo's self-talk ('I'm just a loser') not connected to stakes.
calls.one_drill: Gold moment drill: when a parent discloses personal history, STOP — 'Sophie, pause — you just said you had a hard time as a teenager. What happened for you? Because I think that's exactly why you're here today.'
calls.one_line: The parent's story is usually the son's story in 15 years — if she gives you hers, take it.

calls.key_strengths: Strong NDIS pivot — identified and resolved funding objection immediately. Good situational discovery of complex profile (autism/ADHD/ID). Warm natural rapport. Effective 'what if nothing changes' question. Clean close execution.
calls.critical_misses: Emotional labeling weak — never named Leanne's fear or isolation. Pre-sold mum = coaching opportunity missed (didn't test assumptions or earn the close). Impact not cycled before close. Pain stack used program language not mum's own words.
calls.one_drill: Easy close drill: for pre-sold mums, always test — 'Leanne, before I run you through the program, what's the one thing that scares you most if we don't get Matt the right support right now?'
calls.one_line: Easy closes don't mean good calls — always find the fear before the close.

calls.key_strengths: Strong impact moment — suicide concern surfaced and reflected clearly. Clean close execution, 10/10 scale response handled well. Good opening frame. Natural rapport and warmth throughout.
calls.critical_misses: Emotional labeling too general — never named Judith's specific fear (terror, loss, grief). Pain stack built in rep's words not mum's own language. Post-discovery transition to program pitch slightly abrupt. Suicide concern surfaced but not cycled back to hard enough.
calls.one_drill: Words in her mouth drill — use Judith's own phrases in the pain stack: 'You said Jack told you he feels worthless — that's not just teenage talk, that's a boy who's silently screaming.'
calls.one_line: 'You said it yourself Judith — you're not just worried about his rugby, you're worried about him being here.'

calls.key_strengths: Strong situational discovery — complex foster care story fully uncovered. Good rapport and casual tone. Frame set cleanly at opening. Genuine empathy at key moments.
calls.critical_misses: Emotional labeling too generic — never named Beck's specific emotions (exhaustion, grief, guilt). Cost objection not isolated before addressing. Husband not brought in or triaged as deal blocker. Impact not cycled back repeatedly. BAMFAM weak — follow-up scheduled for Thursday.
calls.one_drill: Emotional label drill: for every impact moment, name the emotion — 'It sounds like you're grieving the family you hoped to be' not 'it sounds tough'.
calls.one_line: Pain over price: 'Before we talk dollars, Beck — if cost wasn't a factor, is Blues and Greens what Braxton needs right now?'

calls.key_strengths:
1. Created genuine space for Kelly to share at length — minimal interruption in first 15 min, Kelly volunteered deep context unprompted.
2. Strong "fast forward 60-90 days" anchor question revealed clear success vision.
3. Effective program link: "not carrying it alone anymore" explicitly tied to Kelly's stated helplessness.
4. Honest handling of mentor tenure objection ("I'll be completely honest... 13 months, zero mentor departures").
5. Natural, pressure-free close after rapport was established.
calls.critical_misses:
1. BOMB DROPPED, NOT CYCLED: Kelly said "I expect something bad will happen to him eventually because he doesn't want to be here" — Harry acknowledged then immediately cheerleaded ("awesome trait as a mum"). This is the biggest miss on the call.
2. No specific emotional labels using Kelly's language — "carrying a lot of weight" is generic, not Green.
3. "Feeling helpless" acknowledged but not cycled — moved to program pitch too quickly.
4. Opening slightly transactional: "purpose of this call is..." rather than genuine curiosity framing.
5. No explicit Ask Permission before going deeper.
calls.one_drill: CYCLE THE BOMB: When a parent drops an emotional bomb (suicide fear, "he doesn't want to be here"), pause 3 seconds, reflect it back, cycle 2-3 times BEFORE moving forward. Drill: Harry plays parent, rep must cycle 3× on the bomb before being allowed to continue. Do NOT acknowledge-and-move.
calls.one_line: When mum says 'I expect something bad will happen to him,' that's not background noise — that's the whole call. Everything after that is noise until you've sat in that fear with her.

calls.key_strengths: Long-standing lead (since Dec 2025) reactivated with clean BAMFAM set. Son returning from Bendigo creates natural urgency window. Start fee willingness signals genuine intent — Rye correctly identified the trigger moment.
calls.critical_misses: Financial barrier around ongoing subscription fees not addressed — only start fee resolved. No emotional pain stack in notes. Three-month gap in engagement is a trust erosion risk. Scored from rep notes only — transcript unavailable.
calls.one_drill: Pre-call prep for Monday: Rye to map Steffany's 3 fears (son's situation after Bendigo, ongoing cost, will it actually help). Prepare pain-first close with specific link-back to Koban's situation.
calls.one_line: Koban coming home is the trigger Rye has been waiting for since December — this is the close window, do not BAMFAM past it.

calls.key_strengths: Verbal close secured despite wifi connectivity issues. Creative adaptation — directed mum to complete form offline. Showed composure under difficult call conditions.
calls.critical_misses: LUCKY CLOSE — SIEL 3.7 but outcome is Onboarding Booked. Mum was likely pre-sold before the call. No emotional investment evident in notes — high churn risk if Brodie does not anchor the decision emotionally in activation call. No pain stack built.
calls.one_drill: Brodie activation priority: Call Jasmine within 24hrs, anchor the YES emotionally — ask her what made her decide today, reflect it back, build commitment consistency before W1.
calls.one_line: She said yes on a bad connection — Brodie needs to make sure she is emotionally anchored before Henley gets home, or this could reverse.

calls.key_strengths: Both parents engaged on the call — dual buy-in is rare and a strong buying signal. That both showed up is worth noting.
calls.critical_misses: Single-line notes — impossible to assess SIEL technique. No record of pain surfaced, emotional moments, or what stalled the close. Scored from rep notes only.
calls.one_drill: Partner objection-prevention frame: when dad is on the call, Bailey should name it early — address the fact that BOTH parents are hearing this together and use it as a close anchor.
calls.one_line: Dad on the call is an asset, not a blocker — use the dual parent presence to build shared commitment before BAMFAM.

calls.key_strengths: Discovered DCJ funding pathway — turned financial blocker into a structured next step. Shows resourcefulness on the financial objection.
calls.critical_misses: Notes too sparse to assess SIEL technique. No impact cycling, emotional exploration, or pain naming visible. Scored from rep notes only — transcript unavailable.
calls.one_drill: Post-call debrief habit: Bailey to write 3+ bullet notes per call capturing (1) mum pain named, (2) emotional moment, (3) where call stalled.
calls.one_line: The DCJ funding find is good instinct — but name her pain before you solve the logistics.

calls.key_strengths: 1. Strong rapport — natural small talk, name coincidence, relaxed opener. 2. Rich family context surfaced — blended family, birth mum story, ADHD/ODD, husband away. 3. Decent program pivot — linked not carrying it all on your own to her stated pain. 4. Good objection isolation — tested if Anthony said no, would you still go and got YES. 5. Tayla mentioned potentially enrolling second son Chase too.
calls.critical_misses: 1. EMOTIONAL BOMBS WALKED PAST: Birth mum abandonment, resentment, brick wall — all acknowledged and moved on. Zero cycling. 2. Program mention too early — in first 90 seconds. 3. No $111 close on the day — Tayla asked and Anthony let her off. 4. Partner objection accepted too passively. 5. No GREEN labels — every label was generic, never used her exact words.
calls.one_drill: EMOTIONAL BOMB CYCLING DRILL: Pull this transcript. Find the 5 bombs. When mum says it PAUSE. Tell me more. What does it bring up for you. Label it using her exact words. Do not move on until 2+ minutes spent on each bomb.
calls.one_line: The hardest part wasn't what she said — it was what she dropped and you walked past. The birth mum story wasn't background. That WAS the whole call.
